text: The words ‘as best you may’, literally ‘from the heart’, are not found in the Greek.

The Greek here should perhaps be rendered, ‘you know how to pay the same tribute of suffering as your brethren, all the world over’. The idea of ‘tribute’ is probably suggested by the Greek verb used, though it is not rendered in the Latin.
Some commentators would make the words ‘after a little suffering’ follow on the words ‘will himself give you’.
Silvanus is probably the companion of St Paul (I Thess. 1.1).

The word ‘church’ is not expressed, but is evidently meant to be understood, in the Greek. There can be little doubt that Babylon means Rome; cf. Apoc. 17.5. The Mark here mentioned is doubtless the Evangelist, whom a very early tradition describes as the ‘interpreter’ of St Peter.
